coredata.dc:title: Optical imaging and anticancer chemotherapy through carbon dot created hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Min Sil
Singh, Rajendra K.
Kim, Tae-Hyun
Kim, Joong-Hyun
Patel, Kapil D.
Kim, Hae-Won
coredata.dc:description:
Multifunctional nanocarrier-based theranostics is currently considered to solve some key unmet challenges in cancer treatment. Here we report a nanocarrier platform, named carbon dot (CD) created mesoporous hollow organosilica (C-hMOS) nanoparticles, to deliver anticancer drug and to enable optical imaging. The hollow structure was formed by the removal of a nanorod core template, and at the same time, the fluorescent signal was endowed from the heat-treated organosilica network. Thanks to the hollow and mesoporous structure, the C-hMOS effectively loaded doxorubicin (DOX) for cancer chemotherapy. The DOX was released from C-hMOS highly sustainably (over 12days) and pH-dependently (pH 5.0 >pH 7.4). The DOX-loading C-hMOS internalized cancer cells efficiently (>90%), and induced cellular apoptosis including the expression of caspase-3. The treatment of C-hMOS to cancer cells enabled multi-color visualization in vitro, suggesting the possibility of cell tracing. Moreover, when injected intratumorally in mice, the C-hMOS exhibited strong optical signals in vivo along with a high optical stability (over a week). The injected C-hMOS were distributed only a fraction in liver but not in heart, lung, spleen or kidney and displayed good biocompatibility. The DOX-delivering C-hMOS significantly suppressed the in vivo tumor growth associated with apoptotic functions. Taken together, the developed C-hMOS nanoparticles can be a promising nanoplatform for drug delivery and in vivo imaging in cancer treatment.
Statement of Significance Multifunctional nanoparticles that combine chemotherapeutic ability with imaging modality comprise promising platform for cancer theranostics. Here we developed a novel theranostic nanoparticle, i.e., carbon-dot created mesoporous hollow silica nanoparticle, to offer unique merit for this purpose. The in vitro and in vivo findings to support this include: i) carbon dots with 1–2nm size in situ generated discretely and uniformly within silica network, ii) hollow and mesoporous structure effective for loading of DOX at high content, iii) release behavior of DOX in a sustainable and pH-dependent manner, iv) chemotherapeutic efficacy in killing cancer cells and suppressing tumor growth through DOX delivery, and v) carbon dot induced multi-color fluorescence imaging within cells and tumor tissues. These collective multifaceted properties may facilitate the novel carbon dot nanocarriers to be a potential candidate for delivering anticancer drug and non-invasive imaging in cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hollow nanoparticles
Mesoporous silica
Optical imaging
Chemotherapy
Tumor suppression

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of C-dot created hollow mesoporous nanocarriers (C-hMOS)
Examination of optical properties of nanocarriers
Cell treatment and in vitro visualization
Terminal TUNEL assay for apoptosis detection
Bio-distribution of nanocarriers and in vivo acute toxicity
Preparation of hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles
In vitro and in vivo imaging of C-hMOS
DOX loading onto C-hMOS and the release profile
Intratumor injection of DOX-loaded C-hMOS and in vivo apoptosis

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration, showing (A) the preparation steps of C-dot created hollow mesoporous nanoparticles and (B) their applications in delivering therapeutic drug and optically imaging cells and tissues (‘theranostics’).

Characteristics of C-hMOS. (A-B) TEM images of the nanoparticles; nanorod core template (A), and hollow-structured C-hMOS at different magnifications (B). (C) XRD patterns of samples (core, before and after hollow). (D) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm curves of samples (before and after hollow), and the mesopore properties shown below.

Analyses of C-dots generated in C-hMOS. (A) TEM images of C-dots at low and high magnification, revealing the lattice space of the C-dot ∼0.31nm, corresponding to (002) plane of graphite. (B) Size distribution of C-dots. (C) Raman and (D) XPS C1s spectra of the C-dot generated nanoparticles.

C-dot induced optical properties of C-hMOS. (A) Optical fluorescence of the nanoparticles prepared in DW at various concentrations, visualized at two different wavelengths. Intensity also quantified. (B) PL intensity obtained at different wavelengths. Maximum PL intensity recorded at each wavelength also presented. (C) Variation of PL intensity with pH values (pH 3.0–12.0), and the maximum PL value quantified. (D) 2D excitation-emission topographical maps of nanoparticles with (left) and without (right) C-dot generation.

In vitro and In vivo imaging capacity of C-hMOS. (A) Confocal microscopic images of cells treated with C-hMOS at 40μg/ml after 4h. Images w/o C-hMOS also served as control. Three different wavelengths used to visualize nanoparticles inside cells. (B) In vivo fluorescence live imaging of a tumor tissue in nude mouse injected with C-hMOS, monitored up to 7days.

DOX loading capacity and release profile of C-hMOS. (A) DOX loading amount into the C-hMOS measured at different DOX concentrations. Non-hollow form also shown for comparison. (B) DOX release from the C-hMOS at different pH values, showing a two-stage release pattern. The release pattern at an early phase (<48h) enlarged.

DOX-induced toxicity and apoptosis in cancer cells by C-hMOS. (A) HeLa cell toxicity, measured at 24h of treatment of different sample groups. Significant differences noticed at *p<0.05 & **p<0.01vs. C-hMOS only group. (B) Cell apoptosis, analyzed by the expression of caspase-3. Confocal microscopic images visualized and FACS diagrams quantified.

Effects of DOX/C-hMOS on the in vivo apoptosis in tumor. (A) TUNEL staining of tissue samples to check the apoptotic behaviors of tumor cells, revealing substantial DNA fragmentation only noticed in tumor tissues with DOX/C-hMOS. (B) TEM observation of the apoptosis of tumor tissues. N: nucleus, ER; endoplasmic reticulum.

Effects of DOX/C-hMOS on the suppression of tumor growth. (A) Tumor volume changes with time up to 14days. Data shown as the relative value to initial period (day 0). (B) Tumor weights measured at day 14. Representative pictures of tumor tissue in each group also presented. Significant differences noticed at *p<0.05 & **p<0.01vs. control or C-hMOS; #p<0.05 & ##p<0.01 between DOX vs C-hMOS with DOX.

head.ce:title: Optical imaging and anticancer chemotherapy through carbon dot created hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Tissue Regeneration Engineering, Dankook University, Cheonan 330-714, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanobiomedical Science and BK21 PLUS NBM Global Research Center for Regenerative Medicine, Dankook University, Cheonan 330-714, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nanobiomedical Science and BK21 PLUS NBM Global Research Center for Regenerative Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomaterials Science, College of Dentistry, Dankook University, Cheonan 330-714, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Tissue Regeneration Engineering, Dankook University, Cheonan 330-714, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional nanocarrier-based theranostics is currently considered to solve some key unmet challenges in cancer treatment. Here we report a nanocarrier platform, named carbon dot (CD) created mesoporous hollow organosilica (C-hMOS) nanoparticles, to deliver anticancer drug and to enable optical imaging. The hollow structure was formed by the removal of a nanorod core template, and at the same time, the fluorescent signal was endowed from the heat-treated organosilica network. Thanks to the hollow and mesoporous structure, the C-hMOS effectively loaded doxorubicin (DOX) for cancer chemotherapy. The DOX was released from C-hMOS highly sustainably (over 12days) and pH-dependently (pH 5.0 >pH 7.4). The DOX-loading C-hMOS internalized cancer cells efficiently (>90%), and induced cellular apoptosis including the expression of caspase-3. The treatment of C-hMOS to cancer cells enabled multi-color visualization in vitro, suggesting the possibility of cell tracing. Moreover, when injected intratumorally in mice, the C-hMOS exhibited strong optical signals in vivo along with a high optical stability (over a week). The injected C-hMOS were distributed only a fraction in liver but not in heart, lung, spleen or kidney and displayed good biocompatibility. The DOX-delivering C-hMOS significantly suppressed the in vivo tumor growth associated with apoptotic functions. Taken together, the developed C-hMOS nanoparticles can be a promising nanoplatform for drug delivery and in vivo imaging in cancer treatment.

Multifunctional nanoparticles that combine chemotherapeutic ability with imaging modality comprise promising platform for cancer theranostics. Here we developed a novel theranostic nanoparticle, i.e., carbon-dot created mesoporous hollow silica nanoparticle, to offer unique merit for this purpose. The in vitro and in vivo findings to support this include: i) carbon dots with 1–2nm size in situ generated discretely and uniformly within silica network, ii) hollow and mesoporous structure effective for loading of DOX at high content, iii) release behavior of DOX in a sustainable and pH-dependent manner, iv) chemotherapeutic efficacy in killing cancer cells and suppressing tumor growth through DOX delivery, and v) carbon dot induced multi-color fluorescence imaging within cells and tumor tissues. These collective multifaceted properties may facilitate the novel carbon dot nanocarriers to be a potential candidate for delivering anticancer drug and non-invasive imaging in cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: ‘Theranostics’ has emerged as a promising filed that combines therapeutics with diagnostics for the effective delivery of therapeutic molecules while monitoring the in vivo biological effects [1–4]. For this purpose, mesoporous inorganic nanoparticles are considered as one of the most fascinating candidate delivery platforms, due to their high surface and pore volume, tunable pore structure, diversity in surface functionalization, and good biocompatibility [5–10]. Furthermore, for cancer treatment, nanoparticles should be able to accumulate in tumor area through an enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR), with an ability to deliver therapeutic agents in a much localized fashion. Thanks to their unique pore structure and surface functionality, mesoporous nanoparticles can be tuned to load different types of therapeutic molecules in terms of size and charge properties, and to release them in a sustained and controlled manner [11–13].

Among the anticancer drugs, doxorubicin (DOX), derived from anthracyclines, has been shown to be effective for the treatment of breast, lung and brain tumors. However, its short half-life, low solubility in aqueous media, and the in vivo toxicity related with required high doses has been encountered as challenges to overcome [14–17]. One of the most effective strategies is to deliver DOX through nanoparticle carriers [18–21]. Loading of DOX at large quantities within the carriers while targeting to tumor sites through a surface functionalization can achieve therapeutic activity of DOX without significant side effects [22–24].

Along with this strategy to improve therapeutic efficacy, providing the carrier system with diagnostic properties, such as imaging of targeted cells both in vitro and in vivo, has recently been an important issue. For this, the nanoparticles are often combined with organic dyes and quantum dots (QDs). While these imaging generators have been widely used as diagnostics in biomedicine due to their low cost and stable photo-luminescent properties [25–28], they are mainly composed of heavy metal elements such as Cd2+ or Pb2+, leaving a profound concern on in vivo toxicity [29–32]. On the other hand, carbon dots (C-dots) have more recently been reported as a new class of nano-lights for diagnostic purposes [33–38]. C-dots do not present heavy metals or auto-fluorescence molecules in their structure and also exhibit outstanding photo-stability, low toxicity and high aqueous solubility, making them safe diagnostic nanomaterials [39–41].

Therefore, our focus here is to design C-dot-carriers based on mesoporous nanoparticle system to load and deliver DOX for anticancer theranostics. In order to provide a high loading capacity of DOX, the nanocarrier is further developed with a hollow structure, as illustrated in Fig.1 A. Some of the recent studies have reported the significant enhancement of the hollow-structured nanoparticles in the loading of biomolecules and even in their delivery in a more sustained manner [42,43]. The generation of C-dots within the hollow mesoporous network is ascribed to the previous works where the silica was organic-modified and thermal-treated [44,45]. Here we demonstrate that the developed C-dot-induced hollow mesoporous organosilica nanocarriers (C-hMOS) exhibit; i) multi-color imaging of cells and tissues, ii) excellent loading capacity of DOX and the sustained (long-term) and controlled (pH dependent) release in vitro, and iii) significant suppression of tumor tissue growth in vivo when intratumorally delivered, as schematically shown in Fig.1B.

As a template nanomaterial for hollow structure, hydroxyapatite (HA) was fabricated by the precipitated reaction method. For this, 0.6g of hexadecytrimetyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) was mixed with 1.3743g of calcium nitrate tetrahydrate in 60ml of deionized water (DW). Within this mixture, a small amount (∼3ml) of ammonium hydroxide solution (28%) was added with pH adjusted at 9–10. A separate solution was prepared by mixing 1.7646g of trisodium citrate and 0.4752g of ammonium hydrogen phosphate in 15ml of DW. After vigorously stirring for 10min, the above two solutions were mixed. After additional agitation for 60min, the solution was transferred to a bottle held in a stainless steel autoclave, which was then sealed and allowed to react at 185°C for 24h. When the temperature was cooled down to room temperature, the HA was precipitated out of solution by centrifugation at 8000rpm. Next, the precipitated HA nanoparticles were washed with DW and ethanol, and then dried at 80°C in air for 24h to obtain HA nanoparticles for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of C-dot created hollow mesoporous nanocarriers (C-hMOS)
ce:section.ce:para: Using the HA nanoparticle as the core template, silica phase was coated by a sol-gel process with a slight modification of a previous report [45]. 20mg of HA was placed in 200ml ethanol while sonication. 5g of CTAB in 8ml ammonium hydroxide/22ml distilled water was added in sequential order. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) (1:1) were added to the mixture while stirring. After stirring at 1200rpm overnight, the solution was centrifuged, thoroughly washed and dried.

To create C-dots within the silica structure, the dried samples were heat-treated at 400°C for 4h in air, according to the protocols in a previous report [40]. After thermal treatment, the core HA was dissolved by an etching process to produce a hollow-cored structure. 20ml of 1N HCl was added to 0.15g of dried samples in 30ml distilled water, and the mixture was sonicated for 2h and stirred at 1200rpm then centrifuged. Collected samples were reacted with 20mg of ammonium nitrate solution in 40ml distilled water at 60°C overnight. The nanoparticles were centrifuged down, washed completely, and then dried and stored in vacuum for further tests.

The size and morphology of nanoparticles were determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-3010, Japan). For the preparation of TEM samples, the nanoparticles were dispersed in ethanol, placed one drop on a carbon-coated copper grid. To assess the crystalline structure of the nanoparticles, X-ray diffraction (XRD; Rigaku, Janpan) was performed at 40kV and 40mA using Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5418 Å). The specific surface area, pore volume and pore size of each samples were determined by nitrogen gas adsorption/desorption isotherm at 77K using a Quadrasorb SI (Quantachrom Instruments, UK). The specific surface area was calculated by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method, considering the general range of P/P0 (0.05<P/P0<0.3). Pore size distribution was measured by the Barret–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method. Samples were degassed under a vacuum prior to analysis, with a degassing temperature of 300°C for 10h. The zeta (ξ) potential of the samples and dynamic light scattering were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, UK). The hydrodynamic size of nanoparticles was also analyzed by a Zetasizer Nano ZS, dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern Instruments, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of optical properties of nanocarriers
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence images of the C-dot nanoparticles were obtained using a UV transilluminator (Vilber Lourmat, France), with distilled water as a reference solution. The fluorescence properties of the C-dot nanoparticles were observed by a photoluminescence using spectrofluorometer (Jasco FP-6500, Japan). UV–vis filters of various wavelengths were applied in the survey of the 2D topological excitation-emission maps of the C-dot nanoparticles.

To determine the existence of C-dots and the chemical bond status, Raman spectrometer (T6400, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS; AES-XPS ESCA 2000, USA) were introduced. The nanostructure morphology of C-dots was examined by TEM, after dissolving the nanoparticles in HF to remove the silica phase only and to remain C-dots. The size of C-dots and the crystallography were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell treatment and in vitro visualization
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular assay of the C-dot nanoparticles was carried out using human cervical cancer cell line, HeLa (American Type Culture Collection, Rockville). HeLa cells were maintained at 37 oC in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 in α-minimum essential medium (α-MEM; Gibco, USA) with 10% fetal bovine serum (Gibco) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin. The medium was replaced every other day, and the cells were passaged by trypsin after confluence was reached.

HeLa cells were seeded in each well of 6 well-plates at a density of 1x105 cells per well and treated with C-dot nanoparticles (40μg/ml) for 4h to enable cellular uptake. The optical visualization of the C-dot nanoparticles inside cells were enabled by means of confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM; Zeiss LSM 510, USA). The C-dot nanoparticles were visualized at two different wavelengths (455nm and 523nm), and cells were counterstained with 4‘,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI; Invitrogen, USA) to observe the nucleus.

The C-dot nanoparticles were loaded with anticancer drug DOX for further chemotherapy. For the DOX loading, 1mg of nanoparticles was ultrasonically dispersed in various DOX solutions (DOX concentrations were 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120μg/ml PBS), and then kept at 37°C for 4h. The nanoparticles were then centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 5min, and the amount of remaining DOX in the supernatant was analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Libra S2, Biochrom, UK) at 483nm.

For the DOX release study, the C-hMOS (1mg) loaded with 120μg/ml of DOX were used. The DOX-loaded C-hMOS samples were incubated in PBS at 37°C for different time points, and the supernatant was gathered for an assay of released amount. The measurement was continued up to 14days, and the medium was refreshed at every assay for the next run. In particular, the DOX release pattern depending on pH (pH=5.0 and 7.4) was also examined.

For the assays, cells were seeded in each well of 96 well-plates at a density of 1x104 cells per well and allowed to attach for 24h. The attached cells were then treated with different sample groups for 24h; free-DOX, C-hMOS only, and DOX-C-hMOS. In particular, the DOX concentrations were varied (0, 0.5, 1, 5 and 10μg/ml) to examine the dose-dependent cell toxicity. For the DOX-C-hMOS, the amount of DOX loaded was determined to be equivalent to the free-DOX, and thus the contents of C-hMOS used were calculated to be 0, 6.02, 12.04, 60.4 and 120.4μg/ml. After the treatment, cell viability was analyzed by a CCK assay. The cells were added with a mixture of 100μl of α-MEM and 10μl of CCK-8 solution (Dojindo, Japan), and then reacted for 4h at 37°C. The reaction product was then monitored at 450nm using an iMark Micorplate reader (Bio-Rad, USA).

The apoptotic behavior of cells was then alayzed by the expression of caspase-3 [46,47]. Cells were seeded in each well of 6 well-plates at a density of 1x105 cells per well, and then treated with samples (DOX-loaded nanoparticle, nanoparticle only (12.04μg/ml), or DOX-free (1μg/ml)), and incubated for 24h. A caspase-3 (active) FITC Staining Kit (BD Pharmingen, USA) was used to detect the apoptosis induced by the samples. Cells were washed with cold PBS and then fixed with BD Cytofix/Cytoperm™ solution for 30min on ice. The fixed cells were washed twice with BD Perm/Wash™ buffer and then resuspended in the BD Perm/Wash™ buffer plus FITC–Caspase-3 antibody for 30min. The stained cells were then washed with BD Perm/Wash™ buffer and analyzed using a FACS Calibur flow cytometer. The data acquired for 10000 cells in each sample were analyzed using the CellQuest Pro software.

Four-week-old Balb/c nude mice were used to study the in vivo therapeutic and diagnostic efficacy of the DOX-delivery system. Animals were kept under barrier conditions with a 12-h light/dark cycle (lights on from 8:00 to 20:00), with relative humidity (30–70%) and temperature (20–24°C)-controlled environment. The mice were allowed normal diet and water ad libitum. The experimental protocol for all animal care and use in this study was reviewed and approved in accordance with guidelines established by the Animal Care and Use Committee, Dankook University, Republic of Korea. After quarantine, 0.2ml of 1x107 human breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) was injected subcutaneously.

For the assessment of the in vivo real-time imaging of the C-dot nanoparticles in the tumor model, the mice were allowed to run the fluorescent scan at a wavelength of 455 and 523nm using the CRi-MaestroEX in vivo imaging system (Advanced Molecular Vision, USA) [48]. Serial in vivo images were obtained at days 1, 3, 5 and 7 after the injection of samples.

The 16 tumor bearing mice were randomly divided into 4 groups (control, C-hMOS only, DOX only, and C-hMOS with DOX). Samples were administered twice via intratumor injection, when tumor volume reached approximately 60–70mm3. The concentration of DOX was 10mg/kg of animal [49,50]. Tumor volumes were monitored at days 3, 7, 10, and 14. Tumor volumes were measured and calculated according to the following formula [51,52]; size (mm3)=length (mm)×width (mm)2/2. At day 14, all mice were sacrificed and the weight of tumor tissue samples was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Terminal TUNEL assay for apoptosis detection
ce:section.ce:para: For the assessment of apoptosis induction in the tumor bearing mice containing the DOX-loaded nanoparticles, the deoxynucleotidyl transferase mediated dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL) assay was performed using S7100 ApopTag Peroxidase In Situ Apoptosis Detection Kit (Millipore, USA) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Briefly, the DOX-loaded nanoparticles were treated into tumor bearing mice and the tumor tissues were excised at 24h post injection and fixed with 10% neutral buffered formalin (NBF) for 24h. The fixed tissues were then processed under routine histological procedures, embedded in paraffin and sectioned with a 5μm-thickness. The tissue sections were deparaffinized and dehydrated using xylene and ethanol and were then treated with proteinase K (20μg/ml) for 15min. The slides were then washed with distilled water and quenched with 3% hydrogen peroxide solution. The tissue slides were then treated with equilibration buffer and labeled with terminal transferase (TdT) enzymes for 60min at 37°C and washed with PBS. The TdT incubated slides were then bound with anti-digoxignenin conjugate for 30min and treated with peroxidase substrate solution until color development. After the reaction, slides were washed with DW (×3) and counterstained with 0.5% methyl green solution. The stained slides were then dehydrated and mounted with cover glass and observed and analyzed under a light microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-distribution of nanocarriers and in vivo acute toxicity
ce:section.ce:para: For the analysis of bio-distribution of the C-dot nanoparticles, mice were sacrificed 24h post injection. The tumor tissue and representative organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) were excised and washed with PBS (×3), and then the fluorescent images were obtained.

For the acute toxicity test, the liver and kidney excised at 24h post injection were fixed with 10% NBF. The fixed tissues were treated under routine histological examination process and stained with hematoxylin and eosin. Moreover, TUNEL assay was performed on the tissue samples. The stained tissues were then observed and analyzed under light microscopy.

Experiments were performed in triplicate, unless otherwise specified. Data were expressed as mean±one standard deviation, and statistical analysis was carried out using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), followed by a Fisher post hoc test. Statistical significance was considered at p<0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: As schematically illustrated in Fig. 1, the nanoparticles are developed with hollow mesoporous structure. For this, HA nanorods used as a template are sheathed with mesoporous silica (MS) through a sol-gel reaction. A subsequent heat treatment generates C-dots in the MS structure for in vitro and in vivo optical imaging, and then an acid etching of core part finally forms hollow-spaced MS with C-dots (‘C-hMOS’).

The morphology of the produced nanocarriers was examined by TEM. Rod shape particles with a size of ∼40nm×10nm were used as a core template (Fig.2 A). After the MS coating, the core was completely removed in acid to form a hollow-spaced MS shell (Fig.2B). The size of nanoparticles evaluated by TEM was approximately 100nm, and the DLS assayed hydrodynamic size was 170nm (±18nm), which reflects a degree of nanoparticles aggregation in aqueous medium, as generally observed in silica-based nanoparticles [53,54]. The XRD patterns confirmed the removal of the core particle; the HA peaks disappeared after the acid-etching process, showing only a broad silica peak at 2θ–22.5° (Fig.2C). The core template removal was also proven by the EDS atomic composition analysis (Supplementary file in Fig. S1). The mesopore properties of the nanoparticles, including surface area, pore volume, and pore size, were examined by BET analysis (Fig.2D). The nanoparticles before (non-hollow) and after (hollow) the core-removal process were examined. For the non-hollow nanocarrier, the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm curve showed a hysteresis loop (type IV isotherm), typical of MS-based nanomaterials. However, for the hollow one, the isotherm curve showed a large hysteresis loop in the relative pressure region of∼0.45 to 0.9, indicative of the presence of a large cavity in the nanoparticle. The pore size distribution was also obtained by means of BJH method. The surface area, pore volume and pore size of the nanoparticles before and after the hollow structure formation was summarized in the inset. The hollow nanoparticles exhibited bi-modal pore sizes, i.e., 3.4nm and 19nm, which can be assigned to the exterior mesopore size of a shell and the hollowed internal cavity size, respectively. On the other hand, non-hollow nanoparticles showed a typical mesopore size of 4.4nm. The hollow nanoparticle showed significantly enhanced surface area and pore volume than the non-hollow one; 1087 m2/g vs. 406 m2/g for surface area, and 0.843 cm3/g vs 0.426 cm3/g for pore volume. Although the pore volume and surface area may be influenced by the dual sized pore structure of hollow nanoparticles, it is not possible to separate the combined effects of each size. The substantially enhanced surface area and pore volume (2–2.5 times increases) due to the hollow structure are considered to improve the drug (DOX) loading capacity. The generated hollow mesoporous nanoparticles had a ζ-potential of −18.2mV.

The presence of C-dots in C-hMOS was observed by TEM. For this, C-hMOS were treated with HF to dissolve only silica phase, and then C-dots were gathered. The TEM observation revealed the presence of a number of round-shaped ultrafine particles (Fig.3 A). On closer examination, the size was measured to be less than a few nanometers. The size distribution curve showed a Gaussian fitting with an average size of 1.8nm (Fig.3B). The Raman spectroscopy of C-hMOS revealed D and G bands at 1390cm−1 and 1610cm−1, respectively, which is the typical of carbon-based materials (Fig.3C). The XPS observation of the C1s region showed a deconvolution into four peaks; a major peak at 286eV corresponding to C-O bonding, and three other lower intensity peaks at 284.5, 286.8 and 290.1eV assigned to CC bonding, CN bonding and CO bonding, respectively (Fig.3D).

The optical images of C-hMOS prepared at various concentrations in DW, visualized at three different wavelengths, showed concentration-dependent fluorescence signals (from 0 to 4mg/ml), and in particular, over 2mg/ml the signals were intense (Fig.4 A). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of C-hMOS, under excitation at different wavelengths (from 310 to 470nm), are presented (Fig.4B). For all wavelengths, the fluorescent peak appeared and the maximum intensity measured was the highest at 350–370nm of excitation.

The PL stability at different pH values was then investigated. Results showed that high PL intensities were maintained over the pH 4–11; confirming C-hMOS can be suitably used over a wide pH region (Fig.4C). This is an important aspect for the effective theranostics, which requires tracking of nanoparticles with high fluorescence intensities in living tissues, even in low pH environments (e.g. tumors). The 2D excitation−emission contour maps of C-hMOS are shown in Fig.4D. Before the C-dot formation, nanoparticles displayed no color configuration; however, after the C-dot formation the samples exhibited a strong 2D excitation−emission color configuration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo imaging of C-hMOS
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the in vitro imaging of C-hMOS, HeLa cells were treated with of C-hMOS for 4h, and the fluorescence imaging was performed by CLSM. When the concentration of C-hMOS was high (40µg/ml), a level higher than the one treated for therapeutic experiments in the following study, the nanoparticles taken up by the cells were intensely visualized (Fig.5 A); the blue (co-signaled with DAPI), red and green fluorescence signals were clearly revealed in the corresponding wavelength within the cytoplasm around the nuclei, proving an accumulation of multi-color fluorescent nanoparticles within cells. When the treated concentrations were lower (5 and 10µg/ml), the nanoparticles were also visualized within cells, although the fluorescence intensity appeared to decrease concentration-dependently (Supplementary file in Fig. S2).

The in vivo imaging of C-hMOS was then investigated after the local injection of the nanoparticles into the MCF-7 bearing tumor region of nude mice. After the injection, serial in vivo live images were obtained at days 1, 3, 5 and 7 using an in vivo imaging system (Fig.5B). A clear fluorescent signal was noticed in the tumor area for up to 7days, while the intensity decreased slightly with time, suggesting that the developed C-hMOS might be used for long-term in vivo imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading onto C-hMOS and the release profile
ce:section.ce:para: Anticancer drug DOX was prepared at various concentrations in PBS (from 20 to 120μg/ml) and then loaded onto 1mg of C-hMOS. The loading amount of DOX was then measured, as shown in Fig.6 A. For comparison purpose, the non-hollow nanoparticles were also tested. For both nanoparticles, the DOX loading amount increased with increasing DOX up to 80μg/ml, and then plateaued. The hollow nanoparticles showed significantly higher loading levels than the non-hollow nanoparticles; the maximum loading amount was 83μg for hollow and ∼28μg for non-hollow nanoparticle, showing ∼3 times difference.

The release profile of DOX from C-hMOS was then examined, as plotted in Fig.6B. In particular, the releasing medium was varied in pH value, i.e., acidic (pH 5.0) or neutral (pH 7.4) to evaluate the feasibility of the DOX-loaded nanoparticles for the pH-sensitive tumor environments. In both pH conditions, the release of DOX exhibited a two-step process, i.e., an initial rapid release up to ∼12h, and a slow-down thereafter (as shown in inset). The DOX release was highly pH-dependent (pH 5.0>7.4).

The induction of cellular toxicity of the DOX-loaded nanoparticles was analyzed, as shown in Fig.7 A. Free-DOX and C-hMOS only groups were also compared. HeLa cells were treated with each sample group at different concentrations and the cell viability was evaluated at 24h of incubation. In particular, free-DOX concentration was fixed at 0, 0.5, 1, 5 and 10μg/ml, the range chosen to be effective in exerting cytotoxicity. Considering this DOX concentration, the nanoparticles with a saturated loading of DOX (10μg DOX loaded in 120.8μg nanoparticles) were used, and the DOX-loaded nanoparticles were subsequently diluted to prepare 6.02, 12.04, 60.4 and 120.8µg/ml, which corresponds to a DOX loading of 0.5, 1, 5 and 10μg/ml, respectively.

The free-DOX treated cells showed substantial toxicity in a dose-dependent manner, and the DOX-loaded C-hMOS also showed a comparable cytotoxicity level to the free-DOX. The C-hMOS only group without DOX showed no noticeable cytotoxicity over the whole concentration range used, confirming excellent cell compatibility of the nanoparticle itself with various cell types (Supplementary file in Fig. S2A). Interestingly, during the 24h of cell culture, the DOX amount to be released from the nanocarriers should be less than the amount loaded (taking the in vitro DOX release data into consideration, the possible DOX release is 25–45%), even so, the effects of DOX loaded within the nanoparticles were comparable to the free-DOX which treated at higher amount during the 24h of culture.

Next, the cellular apoptosis was analyzed in terms of the expression of caspase-3, an essential apoptotic marker. The cells positive for caspase-3 were visualized under confocal microscopy, as shown in Fig.7B. While no cells were shown to have caspase-3 positive signals in C-hMOS only group (similar to the control cell without any treatment), the cells treated with free-DOX and DOX-loaded C-hMOS clearly revealed caspase-3 positive green signals. The fraction of caspase-3 positive cells quantified by flow cytometry showed 66% for free-DOX and 91% for DOX-loaded C-hMOS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intratumor injection of DOX-loaded C-hMOS and in vivo apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: The in vivo therapeutic efficacy of the DOX-loaded C-hMOS was investigated using MCF-7 tumor bearing nude mice. The induction of apoptosis in the in vivo tumor cells was evaluated through the TUNEL staining analysis, as shown in Fig.8 A. The tumor sample treated with DOX-loaded C-hMOS for 24h showed an increased number of DNA fragmentation (brownish granular areas), which however, was not readily noticed in other groups.

The TEM ultrastructural image of apoptosis induced cells was also analyzed, as presented in Fig.8B. Elongated and fragmented nucleus (arrowheads) and the chromatin condensation in the peripheral parts of the nucleus (arrows) - typical feature of cellular organelles in apoptotic cells - were well noticed in the DOX-loaded C-hMOS group.

The tumor growth with time was then measured, as shown in Fig.9 A. For the control (w/o any treatment) and C-hMOS group, the tumor volume increased gradually from day 3 to day 14 (5 times increase). However, free-DOX group substantially reduced the tumor volume increase (only ∼1.7 times increase). Of note, the DOX-loaded C-hMOS group exhibited almost complete suppression in tumor growth. At 14days, the tumor weight was also measured, as shown in Fig.9B. The average tumor weight was 130–140mg for control and C-hMOS only ≫62mg for DOX only ≫27mg for DOX-loaded C-hMOS.

The bio-distribution of the DOX-loaded C-hMOS was investigated at day 1. Some important organs in the body (heart, lung, spleen, liver, and kidney), and also tumor tissue, were harvested and visualized under in vivo optical and fluorescence imaging systems (Supplementary file in Fig. S3). C-hMOS only group revealed an intense fluorescence signal localized only in tumor tissue sample. For the DOX-loaded C-hMOS, the signal was also visualized mainly in tumor tissue while a fraction of signal was weakly noticed in liver sample. It is hypothesized that a fraction of C-hMOS could be captured by macrophages in the liver, as reported elsewhere [55–57]. The in vivo toxicity of the DOX-loaded nanocarriers was further evaluated by histological examinations (HE stain and TUNEL assay) of kidney and liver (Supplementary file in Fig. S4). None of the organs showed any significant acute toxicity.

Development of multifunctional nanoparticles that can be used both as a detection system as well as a therapeutic agent constitutes one of the most promising fields in cancer treatment. Over the last years, novel nanocarrier system, in which bio-imaging agent and therapeutic drug are combined in a single nanoparticle, has been substantially reported. However, most of the bio-imaging nanoparticles are based on QDs and hence have poor solubility, high toxicity and lack of an intrinsic ability to load drugs [29–32]. In order to overcome these limitations, we have designed multifunctional C-dot based nanocarriers. An important aspect of the nanocarrier design is the ability to load high doses of therapeutic agents. For this purpose, we designed an organosilica based hollow nanocarrier that could incorporate therapeutic agents not only in the mesopore structure of the silica, but also within the hollow region of the nanocarrier. The sheathing of the HA nanoparticle can be controlled by adjusting the TEOS concentration during the sol gel process [45], furthermore, the presence of internal cavity contributes to increasing the surface area and pore volume [58,59]. The hollow form of mesoporous silica nanoparticles with generated C-dot is thus considered as an optimized theranostic platform in the category of silica-based nanoparticles (Supplementary file in Table S1).

The developed hollow mesoporous organosilica nanoparticles showed an intense fluorescence signal only after the heat treatment. Pioneering works have reported the fluorescence nature of the silica nanoparticles [60,61]. During the thermal treatment, and in the course of removal of amino propyl groups (as APTES was added) the C-substitutional defect is created in the silica matrix, which provides a luminescent property [40]. More recently, the concept of C-dots has been established [44], and thus the fluorescent property in the hollow mesoporous organosilica is believed to result mainly from the C-dots that are grown from the C-defects. From the PL spectra data, we noticed that the fluorescence peaks tended to increase in wavelength and decrease in intensity by increasing the excitation wavelength. This is primarily attributed to the different C-dot sizes distributed in the organosilica matrix and the correspondingly different emissive characteristics [62,63]. The high resolution TEM supports the presence of C-dots with an average lattice size of 0.31nm, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite [64].

The multi-color fluorescence imaging of the C-hMOS was then proved in the visualization of cells and tissues. The C-hMOS treated to cells at 40μg/ml exhibited high signal intensities at three different color wavelengths. The concentration of C-hMOS used to visualize cells is in a cell-compatible range. In fact, many optical probes such as QDs and some C-dot nanomaterials are used in similar concentrations [65]. Along with the in vitro cell imaging, the C-hMOS intratumorally injected at 3.6mg/mouse revealed intense in vivo optical signals over a week. The administered dose range (∼mg per mouse) of nanoparticles has also been generally used to deliver nanoparticles for diagnostics and therapeutics [49,66]. The results suggest the possibility of applying the developed C-hMOS for cancer diagnosis in vivo.

For the chemotherapy anticancer drug DOX was used. DOX is a slightly basic with a pKa of 8.3 [67], thus positively-charged at physiological conditions, and is relatively small (less than 1nm), which permits the possible loading onto C-hMOS. DOX-loading results clearly demonstrated the effective role of hollow space. The higher specific surface area and pore volume of the hollow form (vs. non-hollow one) should attribute to the increased DOX loading. Along with the effective DOX loading, the release was highly sustainable. While the initial release within 12h was relatively rapid, possibly resulting from the DOX present at the outer surface (mesopore shell), the subsequent release exhibited a highly sustained rate (over 12days), reflecting the significant role of the hollow structure. The DOX release was also pH-dependent, i.e., acidic condition enabled higher release of DOX than the neutral condition; initially (up to 12h), the DOX release at pH 5.4 (∼42%) was almost twice than that at pH 7.4 (∼23%), and the difference was maintained throughout the test period. In acidic conditions, more protons (H+) generated compete with the DOX molecules in the electrostatic binding with the hydroxylated silica surface, leading to a weakening of DOX-silica binding [68–70]. The DOX loading and release behaviors support the potential use of the C-hMOS in chemotherapeutics in tumor environments [71,72].

The HeLa cells treated with free-DOX or DOX-loaded C-hMOS experienced toxicity at similar level. Although the DOX content loaded within C-hMOS was the same as the free-DOX, the actual released DOX content within 24h should be much less than free-DOX treated; an approximation from the DOX-release data gave ∼30–40% release at 24h. Therefore, it is considered that the DOX-induced cancer cell toxicity is more efficient when delivered through the C-hMOS nanocarriers than treated with free drug form - this suggests the possibility of reducing side effects related with overdose usage of DOX [73,74]. When free-DOX was treated to cells, DOX easily penetrate cell membrane and even to nucleus, distributed in a diffused form within cells, and at the same time a lot fraction of DOX can also degrade in culture medium [75]. On the other hand, the DOX molecules delivered through C-hMOS can be released from the nanocarriers inside of cells sustainably. In fact, when the anticancer drugs were delivered through nanoparticles, the anticancer effects were often shown to be more effective than the case treated with free drug form [76–78]. Although the exact mechanism on this cannot be elucidated in this study, the nanocarrier-delivered DOX may be penetrated into cells and distributed within cellular organisms in different ways from the freely administered DOX, which remains an important area to clarify in the near future.

The DOX-delivering C-hMOS demonstrated excellent apoptotic function in cells. In fact, the DOX-related cytotoxicity of cancer cells is generally known as the apoptotic effects [79,80]. The C-hMOS delivered DOX showed a higher expression of caspases-3, a representative apoptotic marker protein, than the free-DOX treatment. The activation of caspases-3 is followed by a programmed destruction of protein and DNA, a damage of cell membrane asymmetry (external leaflet exposure of phosphatidyl serine) and an eventual loss of the integrity of the plasma membrane [81]. Interestingly, C-hMOS delivered DOX functioned better in apoptosis than the free-DOX. Although the exact mechanism on this enhanced apoptotic functions needs clarification with more studies, the possibilities are the enhanced delivery efficiency, different intracellular distribution, and the release kinetics within cells, all of which may vary the DOX-induced apoptotic effects.

In the in vivo intratumoral injection model, the DOX delivered from C-hMOS also demonstrated apoptotic functions through a TUNEL biochemical assay. As a result, the tumor growth was almost completely suppressed, over a test period of 14days. This is more encouraging when compared with free-DOX treated group, which shows limited suppression in tumor growth; this suggests the effective role of sustainable delivery system based on C-hMOS in long-term tumor suppression. Here we applied the nanoparticles directly to an Intratumoral site because this is clinically relevant such as melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the head and neck and hepatocellular carcinoma [82,83] and also a well-recognized tumor model to evaluate the anticancer efficacy of developed nanocarriers [84,85]. To further explore the C-hMOS for the systemic delivery, modifications of nanoparticles may be needed, such as tethering with targeting moiety and PEGylation [86,87].

C-dot created hollow mesoporous nanocarriers were developed for the anticancer chemotherapy and optical imaging. Through a sol–gel process of organosilica compositions followed by a thermal treatment generated C-dots distributed in the silica networks. The resulting nanoparticles enabled multi-color fluorescence imaging of cells and tissues. The hollow and mesopore structure improved the loading efficiency of DOX and sustained the release profile. The DOX delivered from the nanocarriers exerted sufficient cellular toxicity and apoptosis. The DOX/nanocarriers intratumorally injected in mice effectively suppressed tumor growth. Based on the results, the newly developed C-dot created nanocarriers may be a potential platform for delivering anticancer drug and non-invasive imaging in cancer treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by grants from the Global Research Laboratory (GRL) Program (2015032163) and Priority Research Centers Program (2009-0093829), National Research Foundation, Republic of Korea.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.actbio.2017.03.054.
